---
pagetitle: "EpiAware — composable tools for infectious disease modelling"
description: "A composable Julia ecosystem for infectious disease modelling."
toc: false
page-layout: full
---

::: {.hero}
![](assets/img/logo.svg){.hero-logo fig-alt="EpiAware logo"}

# EpiAware

<p class="lead">Composable tools for infectious disease modelling in Julia.</p>

::: {.cta-row}
[Explore the packages](packages/index.qmd){.btn .btn-primary .btn-lg role="button"}
[Get involved](get-involved.qmd){.btn .btn-outline-primary .btn-lg role="button"}
[GitHub](https://github.com/EpiAware){.btn .btn-outline-secondary .btn-lg role="button"}
:::
:::

::: {.feature-grid}
::: {.feature-card}
<span class="value-icon"><i class="bi bi-puzzle"></i></span>

### Composable
Small packages that each do one thing and combine into joint models, so you
assemble the model a problem needs instead of rebuilding it.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
<span class="value-icon"><i class="bi bi-graph-up-arrow"></i></span>

### Uncertainty-aware
Components propagate uncertainty correctly when combined, avoiding the error
that creeps in when separate models are chained together.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
<span class="value-icon"><i class="bi bi-lightning-charge"></i></span>

### Built for response
Designed for real-time infectious disease analysis under the time pressure and
resource limits of a live outbreak.
:::
:::

## Why EpiAware

Models that integrate multiple data sources give better evidence for outbreak
response than chains of separate models, but building them is slow and needs
expertise across several domains. EpiAware provides validated, reusable
components that combine into joint models while correctly propagating
uncertainty — so you can assemble the model a problem needs instead of
rebuilding it each time.

[ComposableTuringIDModels.jl](packages/composableturingidmodels.qmd) ties this
together: assemble a model from interchangeable infection and observation parts
and it becomes a single [Turing](https://turinglang.org) model you can simulate
from and fit. The other packages provide the building blocks it composes.

The aim is to improve outbreak response, as
[epiforecasts](https://epiforecasts.io) does for real-time analysis, and to
make it easier to follow good infectious disease modelling workflows —
especially under the time pressure and resource limits of a live response.

## Built on Julia

We build in Julia for its type system, multiple dispatch, and automatic
differentiation, and on the scientific infrastructure already there:

::: {.feature-grid .foundations}
::: {.feature-card}
### [SciML](https://sciml.ai)
Differential equations, sensitivity analysis, and scientific machine learning.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
### [Turing.jl](https://turinglang.org)
Probabilistic programming and Bayesian inference.
:::
::: {.feature-card}
### [Distributions.jl](https://github.com/JuliaStats/Distributions.jl)
The shared vocabulary of probability distributions our packages extend.
:::
:::

New to Julia? Start with the [Using Julia](using-julia.qmd) guide.

## Background

In R we built the [epinowcast](community.qmd) ecosystem and packages such as
[EpiNow2](https://github.com/epiforecasts/EpiNow2) and
[scoringutils](https://github.com/epiforecasts/scoringutils), now used by
academics and public health teams internationally. EpiAware is the Julia
equivalent: a domain-focused ecosystem in the mould of SciML or Turing.jl,
with community infrastructure like [rOpenSci](https://ropensci.org) and the
domain focus of [SpeedyWeather.jl](https://github.com/SpeedyWeather/SpeedyWeather.jl).

We are at an early stage and looking for collaborators —
[here is how to get involved](get-involved.qmd).
